text: Last class you said: She said that she would send the minutes.
Now we ask: In a meeting, how do you make a suggestion to the group?

Good morning, everyone. Shall we start with the agenda? We have four items today.
Yes, lets begin. Before the training plan, I would like to raise one quick point.
Of course, go ahead. For the training plan, why dont we brainstorm ideas first?
Good idea. And for the budget? Some participants do not agree on the number.
I suggest we vote. If we still have no consensus, how about postponing it to next week?
Perfect. Then lets recap at the end, and you can assign one action to each person.
Shall we start with the agenda?
Why dont we brainstorm a few ideas first?
Sorry to interrupt, but could you clarify that point?
I suggest we vote to reach a consensus.
Let me recap the decisions and assign the actions.

Hello Doctor Nilo, this is Sofia from the FIFA Leadership in Football program office. I am sending you a short briefing before the meeting next week, because you will chair the session this time. Here is the agenda. There are four items. First, a quick recap of the compliance project. Second, the new training plan, where I suggest we brainstorm ideas together before we decide anything. Third, the budget, and I think we should vote on the final number, because we need a clear decision. And fourth, any other points the participants want to raise. My advice is simple: keep the meeting on time, give everyone a chance to speak, and do not be afraid to interrupt politely if one person talks for too long. If the discussion gets too long on one item, you can always postpone it to the next session. At the end, please recap the main decisions and assign one action to each person. Thank you, Doctor, and good luck. You will do a great job as the chair.

Let me tell you about the meeting I led last week with my compliance team. I was the chair for the first time in English, and honestly, I was a little nervous. I opened the session and shared the agenda on the screen, so everyone could see the four items. For the first point, I asked the team to brainstorm ideas about the new training plan, and I wrote everything down. One colleague started to talk for a very long time, so I had to interrupt him politely and give the floor to another participant. When two people did not agree, I asked them to clarify their positions, and after a short discussion we reached a consensus. For the budget, we could not decide quickly, so I suggested that we vote, and the result was clear. We did not have time for the last item, so I decided to postpone it to the next meeting. Before we finished, I asked everyone to recap their main points, and I assigned one clear action to each person. It was not perfect, but I felt much more confident leading a meeting in English.

Here are the steps of a meeting. First, the chair opens the session and shares the agenda. Then we brainstorm ideas and every participant can raise a point. After that, we discuss and clarify the difficult points. Next, we vote to reach a consensus. Finally, the chair gives a recap and assigns one action to each person.
Shall we start with the first item on the agenda?
Why don't we brainstorm a few ideas first?
How about taking a short break before the next item?
I suggest we vote to reach a consensus.
Why dont we brainstorm a few ideas first?
Sorry to interrupt, but could you clarify that point?
Let me recap the decisions and assign the actions.
